So a few things, adding the configuration property to the runner_class makes sense, although the code is missing so I'm not sure, but the other part where it monkey-patches multiple classes to pass a few strings to a template is not a good idea maintenance wise.

I suggest to instead create a new API endpoint `/api/data_sources/<data_source_id>/link` like so (and do the route registration inside the `datasource_link` entrypoint callback):

```python
from redash.handlers.api import api

class DataSourceLinkResource(BaseResource):
    def get(self, data_source_id):
        data_source = get_object_or_404(
            DataSource.get_by_id_and_org,
            data_source_id,
            self.current_org,
        )
        return {
            'type_name': data_source.query_runner.name()
            'doc_url': data_source.options.get('doc_url', None)
        }


api.add_org_resource(DataSourceLinkResource, '/api/data_sources/<data_source_id>/link')
```

Then in the frontend code we simply fetch the data from that endpoint and pass it to the template when the site is loaded. That way we separate the concerns and don't risk overlaps with *other* extensions in the future that also want to add stuff to the query editor page. FWIW I'm not worried about the extra call to the server since we're not really dealing with a ton of traffic.

It seems that `moment` only produces a valid datetimes for timezone offsets [that currently exist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_UTC_time_offsets).

Specifically:

* `xx:00` offsets where xx is in the range [`-12:00`, `+14:00`]
* some `xx:30` offsets (`-04:30`, `-03:30`, `+03:30`, `+04:30`, `+05:30`, `+06:30`, `+09:30`, `+10:30`, `+11:30`)
* some `xx:45` offsets (`+05:45`, +08:45`, +12:45`)

For any other offset, `moment` produces a `None` value, and takes a long time to process.

So `moment` doesn't handle the timezone I supplied, and was taking a long time to return `None`

I'll change the benchmarks to actually check that the correct result is being returned, in the same style as [Pendulem's benchmarking chart](https://pendulum.eustace.io/blog/pendulum-1-2-0-is-out.html#performance-improvements-when-parsing-iso-8601-strings)

? It's not forbidden to mix up InnoDB and MyISAM tables. E.g. in INSPIRE we have ported several tables to InnoDB to avoid table locking. The only issue is whether this specific table requires functionalities that are available only in MyISAM, which should not be the case for any new table, right? Especially since the final goal is to be ANSI and hence PostgreSQL compatible.

The basic limitation of InnoDB WRT MyISAM is the missing support for fulltext search, which should not be needed in this context and which is now implemented as of MySQL 5.6.4 :smile: 
https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.6/en/innodb-fulltext-index.html

On the other hand, since here we are in the context of an upgrade recipe, which is used to migrate live instances of Invenio, since we always have recommended a certain set of optimization to the MySQL configuration which were ignoring completely the usage of InnoDB, I believe it might make sense, in the upgrade recipe to use MyISAM.

We should though create an RFC about our recommended database configuration.

It might help to look at the kValueHelper function in SBInclinedExponential.cpp. In the inclined exponential case, _ksq_min is used as the limit for k in both res_base and res_conv, so when calculating _ksq_min, we need to take this into account so that the sum of errors introduced through this simplification won't exceed kvalue_accuracy.

In the inclined sersic case, res_base is calculated by the SersicInfo class, which has its own internal ksq_min (determined in a similar way). If we were to calculate ksq_min for the SBInclinedSersic class as if it were only relevant for res_conv, then we'd be allowing for res_conv to have error in it of up to kvalue_accuracy. But at the same time, res_base (calculated from the SersicInfo class) also might have error up to kvalue_accuracy, and so res_base*res_conv could have error up to double(ish) kvalue_accuracy, which we don't want.

The place where the exception is caught is very generic. Can the core (not through a front/backend module or microservice) throw this error? 
If yes, then I think it is fine, but the message should also be more generic like `Request processing stopped due an error. Redirecting to an error page..`, as the error could originate from the core. 
If not, then the message is fine, but I would move the exception handling around the places where the error would occur (ie around the calls to `process()` for the microservices). At first I didn't understand that by _plugins_ you meant the front/backend modules, as in my head microservices are kindof like plugins. Finding and wrapping calls to front/backend modules will make things ugly, as those are scattered around the codebase.
If microservices and front/backend modules had the same interface, then this could be refactored to wrap a single call to their entry point. So, this is not something strict, just a suggestion. 
Placing the exception handling under `run()` is very convenient, but it should not be overused - in other words the following for example, is also very convenient but I would not recommend it:

```python
if __name__ == "__main__":
  try:
    main()
  except SomeVerySpecificError as e:
    log('some very specific message')
    handle_specific_error(e)
  except SomeError as e:
    log('some message')
    handle_some_error(e)
```

Errors should be caught and handled by the callers that triggered their originator. If that level is not appropriate then the exception is propagated up the stack until a suitable level - with the right contextual information for the error - can handle it (the very specific error above is most probably better handled in a deeper level). So, I would first try to find that level, than using the top level for every error. 

Now, for our case, this error is generic enough to fit this place, and I will not object having it there ;) 

But then, I am thinking, why shouldn't we redirect to an error page, for any error that is not handled deeper? ie, why should `SATOSANoBoundEndpointError`s not also be redirected to a page where the error message is shown in a nice/user-friendly way? (This would require we build a template/html-page to display errors)

One more suggestion.  Here's an attempt to make this comment a little more helpful:

-----------

Now that we have our variables in terms of `num_spans_to_keep`, we need to compare span pairs to decide each span's antecedent.  Each span can only have _prior_ spans as antecedents, and we only consider up to `max_antecedents` prior spans.  So the first thing we do is construct a matrix mapping a span's index to the indices of its allowed antecedents.  Note that this is independent of the batch dimension - it's just a function of the span's position in `top_spans`.  The spans are in document order, so we can just use the relative index of the spans to know which other spans are allowed antecedents.

Once we have this matrix, we reformat our variables _again_ to get embeddings for all valid antecedents for each span.  This gives us variables with shapes like `(batch_size, num_spans_to_keep, max_antecedents, embedding_size)`, which we can use to make coreference decisions between valid span pairs.

-----------------

Along these lines, changing `antecedent_indices` to `allowed_antecedents`, `valid_antecedents`, `allowed_antecedent_indices`, or something like that, might be helpful.

For the test case, it has to set the timeout to make sure it does not hang if it uses `handle_request()`. In the case `handle_request()` returned, it will need to check whether the test case has done or `handle_request()` timed-out, in the case it timed-out, the test case will need to call the `handle_request()` again. This behaviour exactly matches what `serve_forever2()` function does.

I believe that you are saying this (and subsequently using `round` and `int` in the `batch_size` method of the ArrivalNode) because the `check_timedependent_dist` (https://github.com/CiwPython/Ciw/blob/master/ciw/simulation.py#L71) requires a float.

I believe it would be neater to sample integers directly. Therefore we can change the `check_timedependent_dist` method to be:

```
    def check_timedependent_dist(self, func, kind, current_time):
        sample = func(current_time)
        if kind in ['Arr', 'Ser'] and not isinstance(sample, float) or sample < 0:
            raise ValueError("TimeDependent func must return positive float.")
        if kind in ['Bch'] and not isinstance(sample, int) or sample < 0:
            raise ValueError("TimeDependent batching func must return positive integer.")
        return sample
```

Thus for arrival and service time distributions we sample floats only, and for batching distributions we sample integers only.

For this to work `check_timedependent_dist` must take in a `kind`, so line 145 of `simulation.py` (https://github.com/CiwPython/Ciw/blob/master/ciw/simulation.py#L145) would need to change to:

```
return lambda t : self.check_timedependent_dist(self.source(c, n, kind)[1], kind, t)
```
